signal: God object: 4207 lines, 38 imports, hosts browser_manager + network_interceptor + dom_handler + cdp_function_executor singletons. All 5 element cloner variants imported; unclear which one is actually used. Direct coupling to every subsystem.

15 module-level singletons (comprehensive_element_cloner, debug_logger, dynamic_hook_ai, dynamic_hook_system, element_cloner, file_based_element_cloner, hook_learning_system, persistent_storage, process_cleanup, progressive_element_cloner, response_handler + 4 in server.py) instantiated at import time. No initialization control, version awareness bypass, untestable design.
evidence_pointer: comprehensive_element_cloner.py:344, debug_logger.py:498, dynamic_hook_ai_interface.py:343, dynamic_hook_system.py:522, element_cloner.py:648, file_based_element_cloner.py:648, hook_learning_system.py:566, persistent_storage.py:95, process_cleanup.py:1023, progressive_element_cloner.py:263, response_handler.py:123, server.py:1297-1300

signal: Five overlapping implementations (element_cloner 31K, comprehensive_element_cloner 18K, file_based_element_cloner 28K, progressive_element_cloner 11K, cdp_element_cloner 14K) with unclear semantics and cross-imports. High maintenance burden, tests must mock all variants, no enforced interface.

hotspot: Dynamic hook system + AI interface + hook learning (3 modules, 60K LOC)
signal: Complex system for AI-generated Python code execution with custom sandboxing (dynamic_hook_system 25K, dynamic_hook_ai_interface 12K, hook_learning_system 23K). AST-based compilation, namespace injection, no access control per-hook. Security risk: malicious/buggy hooks can corrupt shared state.
Oversized cleanup module suggests complex resource lifecycle, likely reactive cleanup patterns (kill on error) rather than proactive. If cleanup fails silently, leaves zombie processes.
evidence_pointer: process_cleanup.py (entire module), server.py uses it from line 47 import

hotspot: Singleton version-awareness mechanism vs. 15 global instances
signal: singleton.py implements port/version/pid discovery to prevent stale backend reuse (fix/singleton-version-aware-backend branch), but all 15 module-level singletons bypass this: they instantiate at import time regardless of version match. New backend can load old stale instances.
evidence_pointer: singleton.py:30-80 (version tracking), server.py:1297-1300, conftest for initialization flow

signal: Multiple OSError catches (server.py lines 349, 463, 472, 479) without specific recovery paths. Bare except potential. Exceptions swallowed without logging in error paths = operability blind spot.

Multiple layers (proxy_forwarder, proxy_utils, response_handler, response_stage_hooks) for protocol/response/staging. Unclear state ordering: which layer runs first? Hooks applied before/after proxy? Untestable without full stack.

hotspot: Network interceptor (25K) + DOM handler (28K) tightly coupled to server
signal: Both live as singletons in server.py and imported from their own modules. No clear interface layer. Changes to CDP behavior or request interception require server.py modification.

hotspot: Persistent storage thread-safety (in-memory only, no durability)
signal: InMemoryStorage uses RLock for thread safety but loses all browser instance metadata on backend restart. No recovery mechanism, no durability layer. Observability blind spot: can't audit past sessions.

Default output dir is ~/.stealth-mcp/element_clones with STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_OUTPUT_DIR override. No quota, no cleanup, no monitoring of disk usage. Large responses silently spill to disk; no telemetry on fallback frequency.

Debug logger is a global singleton initialized at module import time. No ability to change log levels per-session or per-instance. Log output goes to stderr without structured format. Can't mute logs in tests without shutting down logger.
~59% of code untested. High-risk modules (dynamic_hook_system, process_cleanup, singleton version logic, proxy_forwarder) likely under-tested due to global state and async complexity.

hotspot: Browser manager lifecycle (no timeout for close/cleanup)
signal: BrowserManager handles spawn/close/state but no enforced timeout on cleanup. Hangs on close block server shutdown. No observable state for 'is browser closing?'

hotspot: CDPFunctionExecutor (executor of user JS with timeout)
signal: Single executor handles all JS execution with max_timeout_ms = 60s default. No per-call timeout override, no queue/backpressure. Long-running JS blocks subsequent calls.
